rows.description: Tunisian plates are black. The red indicates that the vehicle is used by the police. It is not standard on any civilian vehicle.

rows.indicator: Its either freshly painted or faded
rows.description: Its either freshly painted or faded.

Tunisia uses very French road markings. That includes a white divider, often dashed or solid, with white dashed shoulder lines. There is a rare chance to find a double yellow line. They seem to mainly be in some of the major cities.
Tunisia uses a typical French alphabet, as well as arabic.
You dont see bollards too often, but they are around some corners and some drainage areas. They look super French.

rows.indicator: They often point to large cities
rows.description: They often point to large cities, and will have the road number on the sides. They can also be found in Red or Yellow.
This is the general electricity pole.
You will always find signs in both French and Arabic. Almost every sign points to Tunis. You can also find these road markers along most of the major roads.
All numbers look like XX-xxx-xxx with the first two being the area code. These area codes were taken from Wikipedia, but give a good idea of the numbers to look for.

rows.indicator: a red and white curb in most of their major cities
rows.description: Tunisia uses a red and white curb in most of their major cities.

rows.indicator: also the single side version of this were only one of the top lights is on the p
rows.description: There is also the single side version of this were only one of the top lights is on the pole.

rows.indicator: 116 is the KM you are currently at
rows.description: the 116 is the KM you are currently at, while A1 is the road you are on.

rows.indicator: These KM markers can be found on the sides of the major roads
rows.description: These KM markers can be found on the sides of the major roads.

warnings: Local deterministic extraction used GeoTips text only; associated images were not interpreted, so descriptions contain no image-derived details.
